title: Electrospray mass spectrometry and UV/visible spectrophotometry studies of aluminum(III)-flavonoid complexes

KEYWORDS: Ñavonoids, electrospray mass spectrometry, UV/visible spectrophotometry, aluminum(III), complexes
INTRODUCTION
BioÑavonoids are an important group of plant phenol-
ics, diverse in both chemical structure and suggested
biological function.1,2 Among a few of their important
biological functions in animals are those of anti-
oxidants, free radical scavengers, and metal chelators. In
their role as antioxidants and free radical scavengers,
for example, these compounds are thought to be impor-
tant in preventing atherosclerotic plaque formation and
in protecting against oxidative tissue damage related to
neurodegeneration,
aging,
and
the
development
of
cancer.2,3 As metal chelators, the bioÑavonoids may
play a role in both the bioavailability and toxicity of a
variety of metals. For example, the complexation of
Al(III) by various Ñavonoids has been suggested to
reduce the overload of aluminum in the diet (such as
that supplied by teas), a metal which has been impli-
cated in AlzheimerÏs disease, among other maladies.4
From an analytical standpoint, this capability for
metal complexation has been exploited both for identiÐ-
cation of Ñavonoid structure1 and for elemental deter-
minations.5,6 Flavonoids contain a highly conjugated
aromatic system (see structures below), and therefore,
* Correspondence to: G. J. Van Berkel, Chemical and Analytical
Sciences Division, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Oak Ridge, Ten-
nessee 37831-6365, USA
E-mail: vanberkelgj=ornl.gov
exhibit intense, characteristic absorption or Ñuorescence
spectra. Chelation with various metals causes spectral
shifts that can be correlated with Ñavonoid structure.
The UV/visible absorption spectra of Ñavonoids in the
presence of Al(III) have been used to distinguish Ñavo-
noids that contain either a free 5- or 3-hydroxyl group.
Flavonoids that do not contain these groups do not
form complexes with Al(III) and therefore their spectra
are
unaltered.1
Among
those
Ñavonoids
that
do
complex Al(III), morin, quercetin and kaempferol have
been used in the Ñuorometric determination of Al(III).
In a neutral or acidic medium (typically ethanol), the
characteristic Ñuorescence of the chelate provides detec-
tion levels for Al(III) as low as a few parts-per-
billion.5h7
Despite the widespread use of these metal chelation
reactions, debate continues regarding the stoichiometry
of the Al(III)-Ñavonoid complex or complexes formed in
solution. Experimentation has shown that a free 5- and
or 3-hydroxyl group is necessary for complex formation,
with metal binding apparently involving both this
hydroxyl group and the 4-keto group. In various
studies, employing several solvent systems, complexes of
four di†erent Al(III)-Ñavonoid stoichiometries
have
been proposed, viz., 1 : 1, 1 : 2, 1 : 3 and 2 : 3.5h7
To better understand Al(III)-Ñavonoid complexation,
a method other than spectrophotometry and Ñuores-
cence is warranted. Traditional means to determine
metal-ligand
complex
stoichiometry
and
structure
include NMR and electrochemistry. 27Al-NMR and
potentiometric studies have been used, for example, to
CCC 1076È5174/98/111080È08 $17.50

Accepted 22 July 1998
 ESMS AND UV/VIS OF ALUMINUM(III)ÈFLAVONOID COMPLEXES
1081
characterize the Al(III) chelates of several phenolic com-
pounds found in a tea extract.8 The emergence of elec-
trospray mass spectrometry (ES-MS)9 in recent years
has greatly enhanced the application of mass spectrom-
etry in this type of analysis. Colton and co-workers,10 in
particular, have been instrumental in demonstrating
that the ions observed in the ES mass spectra of metal-
ligand complexes, more often than not, reÑect the ionic
species present in the solution being electrosprayed.
Therefore, ES-MS may be used, at a minimum, as a
complement to NMR or any other means of metal-
ligand characterization. Herein, our recent studies of the
Al(III) complexes of the isoÑavone biochanin-A (1, 5,7-
dihydroxy-4@-methoxyisoÑavone)
and
the
Ñavones
kaempferol (2, 3,5,7,4@-tetrahydroxyÑavone) and querce-
tin (3, 3,5,7,3@,4@-pentahydroxyÑavone) in a variety of
solvents, using a combination of ES-MS and UV/visible
spectrophotometry, are reported.

RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
The UV/visible absorbance spectra of 100 lM solutions
of biochanin-A (1), kaempferol (2) and quercetin (3) in
and
containing 100 lM Al3`, are dis-

played in Fig. 1. The UV/visible spectra of kaempferol
and quercetin, are very similar. In
alone, each
CH3OH
spectrum shows an intense absorbance at D370 nm
(Band I) and at D255È270 nm (Band II). With Al3`
added to the solution, Band I shifts to longer wave-
length (Band Ia, D430È440 nm) and also shows an

(1/1 v/v), were investigated by means of electrospray mass
spectrometry (ES-MS) and UV/visible absorbance spectrophotometry. Electrospray mass spectra of solutions that

relative to all other solvent systems investigated. The
relative amounts of complexed and free Ñavonoid in solution, as estimated by the appearance of the UV/visible
spectra, correlated well with the abundance ratio of the 1 : 2, Al-Ñavonoid dimer to the protonated Ñavonoid
(indicative of free Ñavonoid) observed in the ES mass spectrum of the same solution.

ligand characterization. Herein, our recent studies of the
Al(III) complexes of the isoÑavone biochanin-A (1, 5,7-
dihydroxy-4@-methoxyisoÑavone)
kaempferol (2, 3,5,7,4@-tetrahydroxyÑavone) and querce-

The UV/visible absorbance spectra of 100 lM solutions
of biochanin-A (1), kaempferol (2) and quercetin (3) in
containing 100 lM Al3`, are dis-
played in Fig. 1. The UV/visible spectra of kaempferol
and quercetin, are very similar. In
spectrum shows an intense absorbance at D370 nm
(Band I) and at D255È270 nm (Band II). With Al3`
added to the solution, Band I shifts to longer wave-
length (Band Ia, D430È440 nm) and also shows an
inÑection or lower intensity absorption to shorter wave-
length (Band Ib, D350È360 nm). Band II shifts slightly
to longer wavelength (Band IIb) and shows a lower
intensity absorbance at higher wavelength (Band IIa,
D305 nm). The spectrum of biochanin-A in CH3OH
shows an intense absorbance at 260 nm (Band II) and
only a weak absorbance at 330 nm (Band I). In this and
other isoÑavones, the phenyl ring at the 3-position is
not conjugated with the pyrone carbonyl group. As a
result, Band I, which is associated with the conjugated
lateral B ring, is either absent or considerably dimin-
ished in intensity.1 Upon addition of Al3`, the major
absorbance (Band II) shifts slightly to a longer wave-

length. Band I also shifts to longer wavelength (D380
nm) and increases slightly in intensity.
The six UV/visible spectra in Fig. 1 are nominally the
same as those reported in the literature for these mol-
ecules dissolved in ethanol and Al3`-ethanol mixtures.
Ethanol has traditionally been the solvent utilized for
Ñavonoid structure determinations and for Al3` deter-
minations by means of Al(III)-Ñavonoid complex forma-
tion.1,5,6 The spectral changes observed upon addition
of Al3` to solution have been attributed to formation of
an Al(III)-Ñavonoid complex. There is some debate,
however, over the stoichiometry of the actual complexes
The ES mass spectra obtained by spraying the solu-
tions used in the acquisition of the UV/visible spectra
presented in Fig. 1 are shown in Fig. 2. The mass-to-
charge ratio of the base peak in all three spectra is con-
(m/z 593, Fig. 2a), L \ (2 [ H`)~ (m/z 597, Fig. 2b), or
L \ (3 [ H`)~ (m/z 629, Fig. 2c). In the lower m/z
range of the spectra, two relatively abundant peaks can
be tentatively assigned as forms of the singly-charge
where R \ H~ or HO~ in each case. These ions are
observed at m/z 311 and 327 for 1, m/z 313 and 329 for
2, and m/z 329 and 345 for 3. The abundance of these
Figure 2. ES mass spectra recorded using the same solutions as
those used to acquire the data in Fig. 1. (a) Biochainin-A (1), (b)
kaempferol (2) and (c) quercetin (3). Solutions continuously
Figure 3. Proposed structures of the 1 : 1, and 1 : 2 Al-biochanin
two peaks in the biochanin-A spectrum is much reduced
compared to the corresponding peaks in the spectra
obtained for kaempferol and quercetin. This appears to
be due to more facile fragmentation of these biochanin-
A ions (as well as the dimer ions) under the given set of
interface conditions (oriÐce 150 V). Under milder inter-
face conditions (oriÐce 40 V), these biochanin ions are of
slightly greater relative abundance. Also, an additional
species corresponding in m/z to [AlR(L)]`, where R \
is observed. This species was also observed
under similar interface conditions in the ES mass
spectra of kaempferol and quercetin. In no case were
ions corresponding to multiply-charged, Al-Ñavonoid
complexes apparent in the ES mass spectra.
Possible structures of the 1 : 1 and 1 : 2 complexes of
each of the Ñavonoids are shown in Figs 3, 4 and 5. The
proposed structures are based on the observed m/z
ratios of the ions in the ES mass spectra and on pre-
vious literature reports, which have concluded that
Al3` binding involves either a free 5- or 3-hydroxyl
group and the 4-keto group.1 In the case of kaempferol
and quercetin, in particular, the opportunity exists to
form several di†erent dimer structures (Fig. 5). Liter-
ature reports indicate that binding of the Al3` by the
3-hydroxy and 4-keto group is most favored. Therefore,
one might speculate that structure B in Fig. 5, in which
both Ñavonoid molecules bind to Al3` through these
Figure 4. Proposed structures of the 1 : 1, Al complexes of
kaempferol (2) and quercetin (3).
( 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
ESMS AND UV/VIS OF ALUMINUM(III)ÈFLAVONOID COMPLEXES
Figure 5. Proposed structures of the 1 : 2, Al complex of kaempferol (2) and quercetin (3).
positions, would be the favored structure. Of course, a
number of other considerations, including steric factors,
must inÑuence the actual structure(s) adopted.
Without suitable standards for the dimeric complex-
es, determining the predominate structure or structures
would be expected to be difficult by mass spectrometry
alone. Actually sorting out these structural questions
will require NMR, with tandem mass spectrometry
possibly o†ering data that could support the NMR
determined structure. Nonetheless, the base peak in the
product ion spectrum of the 1 : 2, Al-complex of kaemp-
ferol (2) and quercetin (3) (data not shown) was consis-
tent with the monomeric species [AlOH(L)]`, observed
at m/z 329 and m/z 345, respectively. A peak of much
lower abundance was also observed in the respective
MS/MS spectra consistent with [AlH(L)]` (m/z 313 and
m/z 329, respectively). These fragments support the pro-
posed dimer structures and indicate disruption of the
Al-O bonding that links the individual Ñavones to be
the predominant fragmentation channel. Furthermore,
these data indicate that the ions in the ES mass spectra
of these compounds that are assumed to be the 1 : 1 Al-
Ñavonoid complex may arise in part from fragmentation
of the 1 : 2 dimer complex in the atmospheric sampling
interface. However, the ions corresponding to the 1 : 1
Al-Ñavonoid complex in the ES mass spectrum of
biochanin-A were probably not formed via this route.
The 1 : 2 dimer complex of bioachin-A was found by
( 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
MS/MS to fragment mainly via cleavage of the bio-
chanin molecules rather than via disruption of the Al-O
linkages (data not shown). No ions owing to the mono-
meric species were observed in the product ion spectra
of this 1 : 2-dimer complex. While this is a very limited
data set, the di†ering fragmentation of the dimers of
biochanin-A, and kaempferol and quercetin suggests
that one might be able to di†erentiate isoÑavones from
Ñavones on the basis of the gross features of the product
ion spectra of the 1 : 2, Al-Ñavonoid complex. Appar-
ently the isoÑavone system is sufficiently fragile so that
it fragments at a lower energy than that required to
disrupt the Al-O binding required to split the dimer
Ñavone and isoÑavone isomers are underway to support
If not formed via fragmentation of the dimer, the
monomeric species observed in the ES mass spectra pre-
sented above might otherwise be formed either directly
from ions present in the solution or possibly during
desolvation of a multiply-charged 1 : 1 Al-Ñavonoid
complex. For example, the ions observed at m/z 327 and
341 in the mass spectrum of biochanin-A might result
from the following desolvation reactions involving the

On the basis of the UV/visible spectra and corre-
sponding ES mass spectra presented above, the shift in
absorbance observed in the UV/visible spectra of the
methanolic solutions of these Ñavonoids upon the addi-
tion of Al3` appears to be indicative of the formation of
predominantly the 1 : 2, dimeric Al-Ñavonoid complex.
Additional evidence in support of this conclusion was
obtained by spraying these solutions in negative ion
mode. In this case only ions corresponding to the dep-
rotonated Ñavonoids, formed by dissociation of an
acidic phenolic group, were observed. We would expect
it possible to observe under these conditions the depro-
tonated 1 : 3, AL-Ñavonoid complex if it had been
formed in solution. However, such ions (e.g., [AIL3
m/z 875 where L \ (1 [ H`)~) were not
The UV/visible spectra in Fig. 6 and the ES mass
spectra of the corresponding solutions shown in Fig. 7
illustrate the e†ect of the Al3` : Ñavonoid concentration
ratio on complex formation. The UV/visible spectra
were recorded using a constant 100 lM concentration of
quercetin with 1.0, 10 or 100 lM of Al3` also in the
solution. One notes that little if any complex was
formed with only D1.0 lM of Al3` in the solution. No
clear absorbance was noted at the wavelength (D440
nm) indicative of complexation. The spectra indicate
that a signiÐcant amount of the complex was formed
with 10 lM of Al3` added. Nearly all of the quercetin
appears complexed when 100 lm of Al3` is added
(disappearance of absorbance at 375 nm). When 500 lM
Figure 6. UV/Visible spectra of methanol solutions containing
100 mM quercetin (3) and differing concentrations of Al3½. Solid
curve ¼100 mM Al3½, dash-dot curve ¼500 mM Al3½.
is added, the nature of the absorbance at 440 nm
changes, increasing slightly, but also shifting to longer
wavelength with considerable broadening.
The peaks observed in the ES mass spectra of these
solutions reÑect the same results. In Fig. 7(a), we see at
low absolute abundance only ions corresponding to the
protonated molecule, [3 ] H`]` (m/z 303), sodiated
molecule [3 ] Na`]` (m/z 325), and a dimer that corre-
sponds in mass to [2(3) ] Na`]` (m/z 627). No ions
assignable to an Al-Ñavonoid complex are observed.
With 10 lM of Al3` [Fig. 7(b)], the protonated and
sodiated molecules are still observed (actually at slightly
higher abundance), but not the [2(3) ] Na`]` species.
The base peak in this spectrum is the 1 : 2, Al-Ñavonoid
(m/z 629, L \ (3 [ H`)~). Increasing
the concentration of Al3` to 100 lM yields the spectrum
in Fig. 7(c) in which the 1 : 2, Al-quercetin dimer is by
far the most abundant peak. The 1 : 1, Al-quercetin
complexes (m/z 329 and 345) are also observed. Note
that the absolute abundance of the Ñavonoid derived
ions in this case are 10È100 greater than when the Ñavo-
noid is sprayed directly from methanol alone. Increas-
ing the concentration of Al3` further to 500 lM [Fig.
7(d)], yields a signiÐcantly more complex spectrum.
Basically the signal intensity of the 1 : 2 complex is
reduced by 99 percent. The absolute abundance of the
1 : 1 complexes are little changed from 7(b). However,
the methoxy substituted 1 : 1 complex (m/z 359) is now
observed as well as a number of other abundant ions
not readily assigned as an Al-Ñavonoid complex. The
UV/visible spectrum of this mixture (Fig. 6) also indi-
cated a possible change in the nature of the species
present in solution. It should be pointed out, however,
that the high concentration of Al3` might have a†ected
the ES process, and therefore, the resulting ES mass
We also investigated the e†ect of various solvents on
the formation of the Al-Ñavonoid complexes. The UV/
visible spectra of 100 lM quercetin/100 lM Al3` in four
di†erent solvent systems are shown in Fig. 8. As before,
the maximum absorbance is noted at

A similar set of experiments, with some additional
solvents systems, was carried out using kaempferol. The
resulting UV/visible spectra and corresponding ES-MS
results are shown in Figs 10 and 11, respectively. These
results, as all of those above, demonstrate the general
correspondence among the UV/visible spectra and ES
mass spectra. These results also demonstrate that the
degree of dimer formation is not only dependant on the
solvent but on the Ñavonoid. In contrast to the results
for quercetin [Figs (8) and (9)], we observed that 1 : 2,
Al-Ñavonoid dimer formation was major for kaempferol
(1/1 v/v). This data would imply
that the binding of Al3` by kaempferol is stronger than
that of quercetin in this solvent system. The UV/visible
v/v) indicates that a much smaller fraction of the dimer
is formed. In fact, the ES-MS results show the proto-
nated molecule to be of greater relative abundance than
the dimer with these solvents. Note that the overall UV/
visible signal is attenuated in

Electrospray mass spectra of a variety of solvent
contain Al-Ñavonoid complexes show the stoichiometry
of the major complex formed to be 1 : 2. Both the ES
mass spectra and UV/visible spectra demonstrated that
the degree of complex formation was dependent on the
Al3` : Ñavonoid concentration ratio, on the solvent
system employed, and on the Ñavonoid under study. In
favored formation of the 1 : 2 dimer,
whereas dimer formation was suppressed in CH3CN
and all other solvents investigated. In all solvents, the
1 : 2 complex was predominant except when the solution
concentration of Al3` was several times that of the Ña-
vonoid. In these cases, ions corresponding in m/z to the
